DPRK Screening
SecondFi Incident
Memes ↗
Connect
Solana
Forensic analysis · Cardano mainnet · case TIBANE‑2026‑06
It wasn't
nonce reuse
.
It was
Ed25519 done wrong
.
Between 21–23 June 2026, an attacker drained thousands of wallets created by the SecondFi (formerly
Yoroi
) Cardano wallet. Across X and the press the cause is being called “weak randomness” or “nonce reuse.” That framing is wrong — and the truth is worse: the signer computed its Ed25519 nonce off-spec, so a
single transaction
is enough to recover the private key.
Mechanism report
Supply-chain report
Share on X
Investigation 2026-06-25 · all cryptographic claims verified against live on-chain data
Self-check
Check if your wallet is affected
Paste a Cardano payment address (
addr1…
). We pull its recent on-chain history from public Cardano chain data and test each signature for the predictable nonce — the cryptography runs entirely in your browser. No keys, no seed phrase; only the public address is sent.
Check
Public address only · crypto runs locally · chain data via Koios
The finding
Correct Ed25519 (RFC&nbsp;8032) derives each signature's secret nonce
r
from a
per-key secret prefix
mixed with the message. SecondFi's signing code lost that secret on the way through a stack of wrapper functions — the layer that hands the key to the signer simply never passed it along — so the wallet effectively computed
r = SHA‑512(M)
from the transaction body alone. That makes
r
public
and
key-independent
— and once the nonce is public, the private key falls straight out of the standard signature equation. No second signature, no statistics, no “reuse.” Every wallet that signed even one transaction with the affected build — live from
8 June 2026
— leaked its key the moment it signed.
01
Why “nonce reuse” is the wrong label
The two bugs look alike in the textbook — both leak the key through the nonce
r
— but they are operationally very different.
Nonce reuse is the bug that famously broke the
PlayStation&nbsp;3
: Sony signed its firmware with the same fixed ECDSA nonce every time, and in 2010 that let researchers recover the console's master signing key from just two signatures. Ed25519 was designed specifically to make that mistake
impossible
— it derives the nonce
deterministically
, from a per-key secret combined with the message, so a correct implementation can never repeat or expose it. That guarantee holds perfectly —
as long as Ed25519 is implemented correctly
. SecondFi's signer wasn't: it dropped the very secret that makes the scheme safe.
≈
Classic nonce reuse
Requires
two
signatures that happen to share the same
r
across different messages.
In
randomized
schemes like ECDSA, a faulty RNG repeats the nonce — the PS3 case. Ed25519 is deterministic; there is no RNG to break.
Recovery is a difference of two equations:
a = (s₁−s₂)·(H₁−H₂)⁻¹
.
An address that signed
only once
is generally safe.
!
What actually happened
The nonce omits the
secret prefix
entirely:
r = SHA‑512(M)
.
r
is computable by
anyone
from the public transaction body.
Recovery needs
one
signature:
a = (s−r)·H⁻¹
.
An address that ever signed
once
is already compromised — forever.
So while the
symptom
(a recoverable key via
r
) resembles nonce reuse, the
defect
is a specification error in the Ed25519 implementation, not a one-off randomness slip. It is strictly worse, and it cannot be outrun by signing carefully afterwards.
02
From spec to single-signature recovery
For message
M
(on Cardano, the blake2b-256 hash of the exact transaction body), base point
B
, group order
L
, and key pair
(a, A = a·B)
.
RFC 8032 — how it should sign
r
= SHA-512(
prefix
‖ M) mod L
# prefix = per-key SECRET
R
= r·B
H
= SHA-512(R ‖ A ‖ M) mod L
s
= (r + H·a) mod L
Because
prefix
is secret and unique per key,
r
is unpredictable and never repeats. That secrecy is the entire security argument.
SecondFi — the bug
r
= SHA-512(
M
) mod L
# prefix dropped — r is PUBLIC
Two consequences follow at once.
(a)
r
is public — anyone recomputes it from the transaction body.
(b)
r
is key-independent — for a given
M
,
every
affected key emits the identical
R
. We observed two distinct victim keys produce the same
R = 5666e273…
for one transaction: a ~2
−255
event under correct signing.
The key drops out of one signature
a
= (s −
r
) · H⁻¹ mod L
# r now known ⇒ everything is public
Everything on the right is public, so the private scalar
a
is fully determined by a single on-chain signature.
Detector · no false positives
SHA-512(M) · B == R
A signature is affected
iff
this holds. It needs only one signature's
R
and the message
M
— no private key, no second transaction. Run over mainnet, it enumerates every still-exposed SecondFi address and never fires on a correct RFC&nbsp;8032 signature.
03
Where the secret was lost
The signer isn't one function — it's a stack of wrappers (
Secret → Key → derive/sign → ed25519 → noble
), and the secret half of the key falls out at one specific boundary. Not in derivation, and not in the nonce routine.
✓ intact
1 · Derivation (BIP32-Ed25519)
Produces the full extended secret
kL ‖ kR
. Both halves exist here —
kL
is the signing scalar,
kR
is the secret nonce-prefix.
✕ kR lost here
2 · The key adapter
Converts the extended secret into the object the signer consumes. It carries
scalar = kL
— so addresses and signatures still validate — but
never assigns
the prefix.
kR
is simply left out.
adapt(kL ‖ kR) → { scalar:
kL
, prefix:
∅
, pointBytes: A }
# kR is never wired in
— correct
3 · noble's ed25519 signer
The nonce routine
hashDomainToScalar(prefix, M)
is correct and unchanged — it faithfully hashes whatever prefix it is handed. It is handed an empty one, so
r = SHA‑512(M)
. The library isn't the bug; the wiring is.
The on-chain bytes settle which mistake it was: the nonce is
SHA‑512(M)
with
zero bytes prepended
— a zero-
length
prefix, not a zero-
valued
one. That is exactly what an unassigned prefix produces, and it is impossible if
kR
were present-but-ignored or present-but-zeroed. The secret was never wired in — not merely mishandled.
In one sentence: when the wallet adapts a Cardano
kL ‖ kR
extended key into noble's signer, it passes
kL
as the scalar and leaves the nonce-prefix empty, so
kR
never reaches the signature.
04
Verified on live mainnet keys
Proof, not speculation.
a·B == A
We recovered victim private scalars from individual historical signatures and confirmed each by regenerating the on-chain public key. The recovered scalar reproduces the published key — definitive proof of compromise. Scalars are retained as forensic evidence only and were never used to build or submit a transaction.
fingerprint = authorship
Because only the buggy signer leaves the fingerprint while the attacker signs with a correct signer, the same test separates legitimate activity from attacker activity: the drains are exactly the first
clean-signed
spends from keys whose entire prior history is fingerprinted.
05
How it reached production
The deeper story isn't the math — it's governance. The broken signer wasn't a third-party dependency that slipped in; it was EMURGO's own in-house code.
The signer was EMURGO's own in-house multi-chain SDK — known internally as
Dullahan
and published to npm as
@stashers.io/trantor
by its original author, a former EMURGO employee. EMURGO's own copyright-takedown filing names the code as its proprietary Dullahan project — a name that appears verbatim in the published source — and the author's counter-filing disputes only its licensing, not its origin. Only one pre-1.0 version (
0.5.2
) was ever published, with no publicly known audit and a Catalyst proposal to fund one that was never funded. In SecondFi's June rewrite this SDK
replaced EMURGO's established Rust signing library
(
cardano-serialization-lib
), which had been signing correctly, and went live on
8 June 2026
(build 10.0.3, never publicly tagged) — the day the first predictable-nonce signature appears on-chain.
~
The error · developer
A genuine mistake — a wrong nonce in the signer, with an audit pending (the author's Catalyst proposal sought one).
The “hack / unfinished” descriptions are the author's own
post-incident words
; the package itself carried no such warning.
Ordinary pre-release risk — unaudited, pre-1.0 code is allowed to be wrong. (noble is exonerated.)
!
The incident · deployer
A co-founding Cardano entity shipped its
own in-house SDK to production
, replacing an established signer that was working correctly.
No independent audit — the only path to one (an unfunded Catalyst proposal) was never voted on.
No produced-signature self-test, no differential test against the outgoing Rust signer.
We obtained the shipped Android build (10.0.3), extracted the Hermes bundle, and decompiled it: the live
sign()
maps
line-for-line
onto trantor's published 0.5.2
curve.ts
,
221 of 248
(89%) of its distinctive identifiers are present, and the app is
com.emurgo
— EMURGO's own. (We can't pin the exact shipped version; 0.5.2 is the basis of what ran, and the vulnerable
sign()
is unchanged between them.)
@noble/curves
is
not
at fault — trantor only borrows noble's curve arithmetic; the broken nonce lives in trantor's own hand-written wrapper.
The cryptographic mistake — a genuine bug in unaudited, pre-1.0 code — is ordinary pre-release risk. The incident was a governance failure: a co-founding Cardano entity promoted its own unvetted in-house signer over an established library that was working, when a single test vector, signature self-check, or diff against the outgoing implementation would each have caught it.
Read the supply-chain analysis →
06
If you used SecondFi / Yoroi
What this means for you.
The rule of thumb: did you sign on or after 8 June 2026?
On-chain measurement shows fingerprinted signatures exist
only between 8 June 2026 and the patch
— none before. If your wallet signed any transaction in that window, treat its keys as
exposed
. Transactions before that date used the correct, audited signer and are not exposed by this flaw. Re-importing the same recovery phrase into a different wallet does
not
help — the exposure is at the key level, not the app level.
Move to a freshly generated key
Generate a brand-new key in a known-good wallet and move funds there with a single signature, accepting that the old key is burned. Severely affected balances need coordinated rescue.
For wallet engineers: the boundary is where keys die
Tellingly, the core primitive here was a vetted library (noble) — the leak was in the hand-written
adapter
that fed it key material and silently dropped half the secret. Using a good library is necessary but not sufficient: the code that marshals key material into it must preserve the whole secret. The fix is not to bolt on a check for this one fingerprint — that catches a single mistake and says nothing about the countless other ways a bespoke key path leaks. Treat key handling and nonce derivation as specialist crypto code, not glue, and prove with test vectors that what the signer receives is exactly what derivation produced.
Two companion reports
The
mechanism advisory
covers the cryptography, timeline, fund-flow reconstruction, and a reproducible methodology against the public Koios API. The
supply-chain analysis
covers how the unaudited SDK reached production, with the decompiled-build attribution.
Mechanism (PDF)
Supply chain (PDF)
Share on X
Independent analysis by Tibane Labs. Aggregate loss figures are OSINT-sourced; the cryptographic mechanism and deployed-code attribution were independently verified.
